does: This paper investigates what actually changes in large language models during Supervised Fine-Tuning by using Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) as a diagnostic tool. While cosine similarity of hidden activations remains high after SFT, suggesting minimal change, the authors demonstrate that projecting these same activations through pretrained SAEs reveals substantial divergence in the underlying sparse latent representations. They develop an analytical pipeline leveraging SAEs to identify which specific semantic features are systematically altered during fine-tuning, discovering task-specific and layer-specific patterns of feature modification. Notably, they identify distinct layer-wise update profiles associated with safety alignment, providing mechanistic insights into how SFT reshapes model computations at the level of interpretable sparse features rather than raw activation geometry.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in mechanistic interpretability using sparse autoencoders, which have emerged as tools for decomposing neural network activations into interpretable features. It responds to earlier observations that SFT causes surprisingly small changes in activation similarity, which suggested the fine-tuning process might be relatively shallow. The paper extends research into understanding alignment and safety fine-tuning by moving beyond aggregate metrics like loss and accuracy to examine feature-level changes. It connects to broader literature on how neural networks adapt during training and fine-tuning, particularly work exploring whether such adaptation involves learning new features or reweighting existing ones. The use of pretrained SAEs to analyze downstream models relates to recent interpretability research investigating how sparse representations transfer across model variants.
enables: This work establishes SAEs as a high-resolution lens for understanding fine-tuning dynamics, enabling future research to apply similar diagnostic approaches to other training procedures like reinforcement learning from human feedback, continual learning, or domain adaptation. The discovery of layer-specific and task-specific feature modification patterns suggests concrete targets for controlled intervention studies—researchers could selectively modify identified features to test their causal role in model behavior. The identified safety alignment signatures could inform better understanding of alignment mechanisms and potentially guide development of more efficient safety training methods. This mechanistic foundation opens pathways for predicting which features will change under different fine-tuning objectives, designing more targeted fine-tuning procedures, or detecting when models may have learned unsafe behaviors.
